speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came to-- then I told the-- you are with such a rich family. You ask me what I'm doing? I have an apartment. And I got a-- She said, Simon, please, maybe they have an apartment for me also. I don't want to stay with my family. I don't want to be with that family. I don't want to be there. I cannot be there. All right. Come over. We will go over, you're going to-- and she found it, in the same building. I was, let's say, the third floor. She was on the fourth floor, and she moved in. And we had the whole building there, was just the newcomers, when they came. They moved them in that-- that building. And we lived there. Then I-- you know-- it's not just to live, you have to eat also. Not just live. Finally, I found a place where to work, and I make $0.75 an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cutters. I cut up shoes. I work there for-- this was year-- this was in the beginning-- this was in '46. And Helen, you know, expected a baby. I worked, and finally in October, she went to the [NON-ENGLISH] hospital, and she had a little girl. [LAUGHS] This little girl, our little girl, is really-- I wish all my friends to have such a little girl as she. She is now a psychiatrist-- psychologist. Her daughter is going to be lawyer. It's this-- She was born there in '46, then I have another one-- this is Sheila. She was born in '49. I lived in a cold flat here-- in that flat-- and there was no toilet in the house, was toilet outside. The older one, Debbie, was-- you know-- I put in a coal stove, an oil burner, because she was so trained. We sort of-- if somebody came up, she said, no no, no-- shh-- hot, hot, hot, hot. So nobody should go over to that stove. And we moved out from there, and I got sick. And I leave them for almost for a year, I left them-- left for-- she was born, was, at that time, remember to Denver-- I had to be in Denver. That's what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your lungs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For almost a year, I'd been away from the-- from the children, from the two little ones. And thank God, I came back. I start to work. Helen helped a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: We built a nice home, you see? This is-- remember I tell you, living in that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: This girl, what-- look at her. She was in Vietnam, she was in Thailand, she was in Turkey-- she was all over with Bob Hope, and-- and Dean Martin. She was with the gold diggers. She was a gold digger. They married off. I have two son-in-laws-- better results-- they're better. They're so good to me, because they're good to their wives. You know, they have a nice family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family had a name. If they want to say-- a sample from the family, they said, you look at the Friedman's family. You look at Friedman's daughters. Are they-- you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: But God-- it happened. You know, my-- the wife got sick, she is three years already in a home, a nursing home. Three years, already. She knows me. I'm coming. They come in constantly. They come in to visit their mother. But they cannot live here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dad, you want to come with us? Come live with us, everything. No, I said, no. She-- already come-- she is leaving, the other one is coming. So I lived in New York. I bought a house in New York and I work. You know-- I had a-- own a house in Brooklyn, and brought up the children nicely. A nice family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going through that, everything-- you know, but pointing the finger, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Thank God I don't have-- [INAUDIBLE]. American was wonderful to me. I was-- I worked hard my life here. I worked very hard. Not from the hard-- from the work. I've been a cab driver for 18 years here, in New York. I drove the cab for the family, keep the family together. Make nice weddings, and I'm-- and I'm proud of my children. They-- they kept me alive. She had a nice home. The other one, a nice home. They live in there-- nice positions for the son-in-laws, nice positions for the girls. Have nice grandchildren. I have four grandchildren-- for Poppies-- everything. They coming-- they-- the [INAUDIBLE] of the 50th anniversary last year here, in-- in-- Florida. I took out my wife from the home, in the Palmier Hotel. I invite some people. We had a beautiful-- a very beautiful, fine-- but she was sick. Brought her and the two nurses. So I have two nurses for her. One nurse works in the morning until the afternoon, from the afternoon, the second. Every single day-- if, at all the time, when I'm not there, either I am sick, you know-- I'm not in the town. But, besides this, she's expecting me every day. If I'm coming in, she see-- my husband is here. That's what she-- that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you one more question, something we didn't talk about. You told me that you testified at several trials.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk just briefly about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: During this time when I were here, they found out I was in Treblinka. And they start, from Justice Department, here from Washington, they start to send out people because they arrested some people. They want to find out, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first time is by Lanz. They called Lanz in Vienna. He was sitting-- he was from Vienna. He was sitting-- this is the rumor, and that's what I tell you. They told me, and that's what I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewee
text: If this is true, that, I don't know. They told me that Lanz was sitting in the restaurant and drinking beer. As he was drinking beer, he start to give up his credentials, what he did during the war. Said that, I killed the Jews in Treblinka. Oh, Treblinka's my-- some American soldier was there. He went out and he notified some MP, and they came, they arrested him. This was Lanz, the head. He knocked out from my mouth two teeth. I was-- when I told you, I was working-- I was not a carpenter. I didn't know it. I was standing, and I smoked. As I was smoking the cigarette one morning, I was standing at work, and I was smoking.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came over, I took it and-- you know. Then he saw some smoke near my table. He came over to me and said, you smoke? I said, no. When I said, no, he-- one on this side, and one on the other side. Are you a smoker? I said, no. I hear the guys in the back, they tell me [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went away. And they got him. I have to tell him that everything-- witness. I came to-- to Vienna, and I had this picture with me. I came over in front of the-- of the George, and I said to George, [NON-ENGLISH], this is not the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was going to tell you, I lived through that war. This is the Friedmans. See, because I looked good. This is the Friedman. That it was a jury there, was sitting in the jury. So they took a picture, and everyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the lawyer-- his lawyer, after my testimony, what I told him, he said, stand in front of the jurors and said, this man tells you a nice story. A story! I tell a story! So this is for you a story? This is the reality. That's the way I lived. This is the way how he hit me. This is-- is this a story? He got 10 years. I have the paper. Do you see the paper? 10 years, Lanz got.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finish with them, they have them [NON-ENGLISH]-- a murderer, a murderer. They got him, here from Treblinka. [SPEAKING POLISH]. We went to Dusseldorf. I went to Dusseldorf, and he came to the court and in court I said, George, look, I lived through this as a Jew. I want to give my testimony in Yiddish. And I did that. I did the whole testimony, I gave it in Yiddish. It was somebody-- says there right away, interpreted. This is it. Then they came with a bunch of pictures. I should recognize somebody. And I looked at the picture and I see it. I know this-- I don't know the name. I forgot. I don't know the name, but I know the picture. The [INAUDIBLE] view.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago, this is in Chicago. So I recognize the picture, though, they called me as-- as a witness. I've been in Chicago, in the cold. And I told them how that-- everything. He was not at that court. His spy was at that court.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so the court was also called up. This, what I recognize the guy from the picture. I said he's in South America, you know? They didn't want me to get to the court, that I was-- after my testimony that he-- I said to them, what are you doing? He said I'm here from yesterday on. So he-- I don't know he-- how much-- what kind of-- I heard also they got but 10 years. They got 10 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I have-- four weeks ago, five weeks ago, they came to my house, again, with a bunch of pictures. I should recognize them, just five weeks, not even five weeks. I looked at the picture-- that's still 50 years, more than 50 years. You can't-- I didn't know if I would recognize my mother. I looked at the picture, and I stopped by one. They said, we're going to let you-- if I'm going to be ready to go up, there's a trial. Certainly, it's my duty, it's my obligation. Not because I want to go-- I must go. I must tell them how they-- everything. This is the trials which I avoided that before, and I didn't tell you this, that's what-- there were three trials when I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think gave you strength, helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm a Jew, that's why. I want-- you see, I'm not saying the commandants, and I-- I have a certain satisfaction. When I know it, and there was a Nuremberg trial-- they hanged them, that-- everything. What a satisfaction that. Oh, look-- living through so much, so much I still have-- I-- I lived, and I brought up a family, a nice family, and good family name, and I'm going on. I'm going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what did you get from your experience that you tried to give to your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them to be Jews-- good Jews. You don't have-- I said to them, you don't have to be-- who said that wearing the payots, the [NON-ENGLISH] and everything, to be a Jew? If you a Jew in your heart, this is enough. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- I feel they are good kids-- good Jews. They're not Hasidic. I'm not a Hasid. I came from a very, very religious home.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father was-- you know, he didn't do anything, not to ask the rabbi what to do, but what he should do it. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them, I told them from the beginning, as soon as they start to understand, I told them how I lived, where I was, what has happened to me and everything. They know-- they know better than I know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She belong-- the other one belongs to do the HALAT. I have-- I will show you a film here, this house. When I was interviewed, she was in Cincinnati. She was living in Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the [NON-ENGLISH], that nobody have at that time. So she knew that I'm going to come there. So she told the people from the show, they know she's a second generation child. So she told them, oh, my father is coming here. You want to interview him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have the movie-- the video here-- here, in this room, the video. They interviewed me in Cincinnati in the television. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: See-- they didn't make-- they-- they are proud, you see? They feel good that I'm alive, and-- and I could do this, what I did to my life until that. Is there anything else that I didn't ask you about that you feel you want to say at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that I did not ask you that you feel you want to add--

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing I want to tell you, you should understand, I cannot-- that's 50 years. When I start to talk about it, that comes to my mind, I want to jump over this and I want to-- that's what I'm telling you this and I'm telling you this. If I have to sit and tell you a day's life in Treblinka, you can sit with me for hours, for hours and I will not telling you everything. One single day in Treblinka. You know what that means to stay outside in the-- in the snow, in the rain and wait for the-- the-- it will come over to the appel. And you have to stay like a-- like a mummy, not to move. You see, if you move, you-- how many-- when I worked in the [NON-ENGLISH], I did something which the-- the-- the-- Ukrainian didn't like it. He gave me 10 over my behind. I couldn't sit-- for weeks, I couldn't sit on my-- not lay, not just the-- No, with a-- with a stick-- he broke a branch from the tree and he gave me 10 laid down. He gave me 10. He counted, 1, 2, 3. It was black. My behind was black, completely black. I couldn't-- it-- this is-- that's what I told you. I cannot tell you everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just to go over-- just to stay, they put in a Jew, they killed a Jew and they put them him in that grave. He was not dead. And he cried out from that grave, [NON-ENGLISH]! You know, you-- you-- you cannot-- you cannot-- think about-- they-- we were standing and we cannot-- we cannot-- we have to sit, take with that shovel the sand and sand-- and bury him. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you, Mr. Friedman for giving us this opportunity to hear what you can tell us in this short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm telling you again and again, whatever I said, whenever I mention it here, there is more hours to sit and talk about that, everything. That's all right. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Cut now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father told us what his message was to you and your sister and to the future. What was the message that you got from your upbringing and your experience as a child in this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message that I got from both my mother and my father was how important family was. And my sister and myself have instilled that in our children, and we know that our children will instill it in their children. Because you never know-- one minute you're a family of six, 10-- next minute, you're a family of nobody, nothing. Never had grandparents. I didn't know what it's like to have grandparents love me. My parents are grandparents to my children, and my sister's children. There are no better grandparents than my parents are and were to my children and my sister's children. We always get together for holidays. We never miss a holiday together. My sister is in New York. My parents are in Florida. My husband I are now in St. Louis. My son is in Texas. My daughter is in California. My niece is in Indiana. My other niece is in New York. There's 10 of us. And any holiday, for Passover for sure, there was 10 of us at the table last year. It was difficult, because mom is ill-- but we made sure that last year there was 10 of us at the table. And if one of us can't make it, there's a picture of them at the table. Dad's with us for Rosh Hashanah. He's with one of us for Yom Kippur. He's instilled love, love and family-- family and love. And there's nothing better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is from my mother. She made this picture because they were forced to make some passports. She had to make that picture. This is before the war-- before the war. She had to have her passport. She made that picture. And this picture, my sister, when she was in Russia during the war, she had this picture with her. And she came back. She make a big portrait out of it and she gave this to me. There was a little one-- there was a very tiny, little picture. My older daughter, to mine anniversary, and my birthday, she wants to surprise me. So she take-- she took that little picture to Saks Fifth Avenue and she enlarged it, and she brought me a gift for my birthday. And she said, Dad, I know you would like that gift more than everything because this is your mother. That's the way I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Feige.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year do you think this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year this picture was taken? I would say this picture was taken the beginning of the '30. This picture was taken in Lodz, on [NON-ENGLISH]. I was in the army, and there was Shavouts, a holiday, Shavouts. I got to free from the army for 10 days. I want to go to see my brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Being there, we was walking on the street and they took a picture on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '31, '32.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother is on the right-- on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm there-- I'm a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the right. Yes. What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yankel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shosha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in the Lodz ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sister-in-law, they took her away with her child. She had a little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? I don't know. I can even tell you where, which concentration camp she was liquidated. I know he was-- he died in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to tell you that my mother died in the Lodz ghetto. She was in the Lodz ghetto. They had a little boy from six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Shloime after my father. This picture was taken from-- a Russian soldier took it. I was-- after the liberation, I was shot twice. As you can see, the bandage in my neck-- you can see the bandage on my wrist. And I couldn't walk that time. After the first-- after the first bandages, they gave-- they did it for me and when I met them in Sokolov, one soldier took the picture. And being there, a day later, two days later, he brought me over and he gave me this picture. This picture is taken after the war. This is the-- in the cemetery, in [NON-ENGLISH]. We exhumed-- we knew where there were buried. We exhumed them from the grave, and we hired a undertaker. He should take out the remains from the bodies, and put them in two coffins, and take it to the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I am here and this is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can you see? No, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the remnants from the bodies where they-- laying around by that-- by that coffin. This is the brother of that woman that is buried here. And there were here about 19 people, was laying in that grave, when I exhumed them. And all the 19 were put together and we buried them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frania-- and the little boy was Shloime. And there was another-- another group of men, which I don't remember their names.
